titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to family, 21 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to family. Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/69

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
21 Nov. My dear folks I really have no
news of moment.
We expect to do a bit of
a move next week but shall
still be in much the same area
as we are at present, though
overlooking a river; but
you won't be much the wiser. I believe we go to quite a
large chateau & rather hope
that I may leave a quarter out,
as the French houses are usually
built with the rooms acting
as passages which is not very
convenient for those who go to bed
early.
Personally I prefer a room to
my own in a cottage rather than
a chateau King's chamber. We now belong to a
of which I must not give you
the number, but Gen' Congrur
is the General & his Chief
Staff Officer is Gen' Grunly
an a Staff College Contempor.
any of mine, whom I have
now to remember not to
call "Bob Grunly". He took
rather a strong line on the
other side during the Ulster
business & very nearly
retired when French left the
War Office in which case he
would only be a Major instead
of a General, but I am glad
he did not do so, especially
as it was only a case of
disappointment at his future
prospects not a matter of
principle. I hope this past disagree —
ment will not affect our
previous friendly relations! I find it hard to write
patiently of affairs in General.
Carson is being proved right yet
once more, & I can hardly
imagine a more fatuous
move than for our 5 old
men Asquith Balfour its to
come over to France & talk
on Military matters without
one single Military officer with
them. The papers have not
commented on this but surely....
Rex's commands seems to vary
from 15 to 700 & I can quite
imagine he is a strong com —
pulsory service man now, so
as to know how he stands. I must end with best
love to all dear folks
& hoping Mal had a very
happy time at Plymouth
& a rest Wilfrid B Spender
